title: Benthic Habitat Mapping
text:

This project outlines the methods I’m studying for producing high-resolution habitat maps by combining field survey data with remote sensing imagery. The workflow below is based on my research into established approaches in the field. I have not yet produced a habitat map from real data — that is the goal I’m working toward.

title: Benthic Habitat Mapping
text:
Why Benthic Mapping
Understanding what lives on the seafloor — coral, seagrass, sand, rubble — is foundational to reef conservation and management. High-resolution benthic maps enable habitat change tracking over time, identification of bleaching or damage extent, prioritization of restoration and protection efforts, and integration with water quality and thermal stress data. I want to be able to produce and work with these maps as part of applied marine data science work.

title: Benthic Habitat Mapping
text:
Planned Methods

Data Sources

Multispectral satellite imagery (e.g., Planet, Sentinel-2, WorldView)
Underwater photo transects for field validation
SfM-derived orthomosaics as high-resolution local inputs



Classification Approach

Object-based image analysis (OBIA) for reef-scale segmentation
Supervised classification using field validation points
Accuracy assessment with confusion matrices and kappa statistics



GIS Processing

Coordinate reference system management and reprojection
Raster and vector integration
Map layout and cartographic output in QGIS

QGIS — primary GIS environment
Python (rasterio, geopandas, scikit-learn) — raster processing and classification
Google Earth Engine — cloud-scale remote sensing analysis (planned)

title: SfM Photogrammetry for Marine Science
section: Why I’m Interested in SfM for Marine Science
text:
Why I’m Interested in SfM for Marine Science
Traditional survey methods struggle to capture the complex 3D structure of coral reefs. SfM offers capabilities I want to develop:

High-resolution 3D reconstructions from standard cameras
Quantitative measurements of structural complexity
Repeatable surveys for change detection over time
Lower cost relative to LiDAR or multibeam sonar

My goal is to build the technical depth to contribute to reef monitoring efforts using these methods, either as part of a research team or through independent projects.

title: SfM Photogrammetry for Marine Science
text:
What I’m Learning
I’m working through the core concepts of SfM pipelines, including:

Image acquisition protocols for underwater surveys (overlap, lighting, camera settings)
Processing workflows in Agisoft Metashape
Quality assessment using ground control points and reprojection error
Output formats: orthomosaics, DEMs, and 3D meshes

title: 2016 Subaru Outback Stereo Build
text:
This project documents the process of upgrading the factory Harman Kardon audio system in a 2016 Subaru Outback into a much more capable, tunable aftermarket system while retaining daily drivability and factory convenience features.
The build centers around a Sony XAV-AX4000 head unit, Maestro integration, a Kicker LX 1300.7 multi-channel amplifier, an active Morel front stage, rear fill, and a JL Audio 10W6v3 subwoofer in a sealed enclosure.
The goal was not just to make the system louder, but to build a clean, flexible, well-documented setup with proper crossover points, gain structure, signal routing, and enough tuning control to make the car sound genuinely excellent.

title: 2016 Subaru Outback Stereo Build
text:
System Components

Source and Integration

title: 2016 Subaru Outback Stereo Build
text:
Wiring and Signal Routing
This build required working around several Subaru-specific quirks, especially because the vehicle originally had the Harman Kardon factory system.

RCA Signal Routing
The final RCA routing uses the Sony head unit outputs to feed the Kicker amp:

Front L/R RCA → AMP 1 input
Rear L/R RCA → AMP 3 input
Subwoofer RCA → SUB input

The Kicker amp is configured so that AMP 1 feeds the tweeters and also supplies signal to AMP 2 for the front door woofers. AMP 3 remains independent for rear fill, preserving useful front/rear fader control from the head unit.


Factory Wiring

AHH-7881 adapter used to connect amplifier outputs into the factory speaker wiring
Adapter ordered with tinned ends for easier termination
Factory Harman Kardon amp bypassed
Subaru telematics/DCM bypass required to restore the factory tweeter path
Factory speaker wiring reused where practical to avoid unnecessary rewiring through the doors

title: 2016 Subaru Outback Stereo Build
text:
Current Tuning Notes

Front Tweeters — AMP 1

High-pass filter: 3.2 kHz
Slope: 24 dB/octave
Filter type: Linkwitz-Riley



Front Door Woofers — AMP 2

High-pass filter: 60 Hz
Slope: 12 dB/octave
Low-pass filter: 3.2 kHz
Slope: 24 dB/octave
Filter type: Linkwitz-Riley



Rear Doors — AMP 3

High-pass filter: approximately 65 Hz
Slope: 12 dB/octave
Used primarily as rear fill



Subwoofer

Low-pass filter: 80 Hz
Slope: 24 dB/octave
Filter type: Linkwitz-Riley
Subwoofer high-pass filter set as low as possible on the amp, around 10 Hz

title: 2016 Subaru Outback Stereo Build
text:
Lessons Learned

The factory amp and DSP need to be fully understood before replacing them
The telematics/DCM wiring can affect the tweeter path
Harness adapters save time, but the pinouts still need to be verified
Active front-stage tuning is worth the effort
Crossover slopes and gain structure matter as much as hardware selection
A good sealed 10-inch subwoofer is an excellent match for this wagon

Status: 🚧 In progress — workflow design and learning phase. Application to real data is planned future work.

title: Reef SfM Workflow
text:
Why This Workflow
My goal is to be able to take a set of overlapping underwater photographs and produce validated, georeferenced 3D outputs that can support reef monitoring, structural complexity analysis, and change detection over time. Documenting the workflow in advance gives me a clear target to build toward and a reference to refine as I gain hands-on experience.

title: Reef SfM Workflow
text:
Planned Workflow Steps

1. Image Acquisition

Camera settings and housing selection for underwater use
Dive survey protocols to achieve sufficient image overlap (typically 80–90%)
Lighting considerations: strobes vs. ambient, color correction for depth
Survey track planning for consistent coverage



2. Processing in Agisoft Metashape

Photo alignment and sparse point cloud generation
Dense point cloud generation (quality vs. processing time tradeoffs)
Mesh construction and texturing
Orthomosaic and DEM export



3. Quality Assessment

Ground control points (GCPs) for georeferencing and scale
Reprojection error review and iterative alignment refinement
Visual inspection for model artifacts, holes, and misalignments



4. Outputs

Orthomosaic (2D georeferenced image) for habitat classification
Digital elevation model (DEM) for rugosity and structural complexity analysis
3D mesh for visual inspection and volumetric analysis

title: Reef SfM Workflow
text:
Tools I’m Learning

Agisoft Metashape — primary SfM processing software
QGIS — for georeferenced output analysis and visualization
Python (open3d, pdal) — point cloud analysis and automation

title: 2026 Super El Niño Watch: Live Thermal Stress Monitoring
text:
Code
```{python}
# =============================================================================
# Cell 1 — Setup: imports, configuration, parser, fetch helpers
# =============================================================================
import os
import pathlib
from io import StringIO
from datetime import datetime, timezone

import requests
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches

# -- Output directories -------------------------------------------------------
_local = pathlib.Path("/Users/renatus/code/el-nino-watch-2026")
PROJECT_ROOT = _local if _local.exists() else pathlib.Path(__file__).parent if "__file__" in dir() else pathlib.Path().resolve()
CACHE_DIR    = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "raw"
OUTPUT_DIR   = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "processed"
os.makedirs(CACHE_DIR,  exist_ok=True)
os.makedirs(OUTPUT_DIR, exist_ok=True)

# -- Site configuration -------------------------------------------------------
STATIONS = {
    "Florida Keys": {
        "station_id": "florida_keys",
        "lat": 24.55, "lon": -81.50,
        "ocean": "Caribbean/Atlantic",
    },
    "Cayman Islands": {
        "station_id": "cayman_islands",
        "lat": 19.30, "lon": -81.38,
        "ocean": "Caribbean",
    },
    "Honduras (Bay Islands)": {
        "station_id": "honduras",
        "lat": 16.32, "lon": -86.55,
        "ocean": "Caribbean",
    },
    "ABC Islands (Bonaire)": {
        "station_id": "abc_islands",
        "lat": 12.15, "lon": -68.28,
        "ocean": "Caribbean",
    },
    "Great Barrier Reef (Central)": {
        "station_id": "gbr_central",
        "lat": -18.00, "lon": 147.00,
        "ocean": "Indo-Pacific",
    },
    "Palau": {
        "station_id": "palau",
        "lat": 7.50, "lon": 134.50,
        "ocean": "Indo-Pacific",
    },
    "West Papua (Raja Ampat)": {
        "station_id": "west_papua",
        "lat": -1.50, "lon": 132.00,
        "ocean": "Indo-Pacific",
    },
}

CRW_BASE_URL = "https://coralreefwatch.noaa.gov/product/vs/data/{station_id}.txt"
ONI_URL      = "https://www.cpc.ncep.noaa.gov/data/indices/oni.ascii.txt"

# -- Alert level classifier (NOAA 5-level system, expanded December 2023) -----
def dhw_to_alert(dhw):
    """
    Map a DHW value (deg C-weeks) to a NOAA CRW bleaching alert level string.

    The 5-level system was expanded from the legacy 2-level system in December
    2023 in response to extreme heat stress events that exceeded Level 2 ceilings.
    Levels 3-5 represent conditions with no historical precedent at most sites.
    """
    if pd.isna(dhw) or dhw < 0:
        return "No Stress"
    elif dhw < 4:
        return "Bleaching Watch"
    elif dhw < 8:
        return "Bleaching Warning"
    elif dhw < 12:
        return "Alert Level 1"
    elif dhw < 16:
        return "Alert Level 2"
    elif dhw < 20:
        return "Alert Level 3"
    elif dhw < 24:
        return "Alert Level 4"
    else:
        return "Alert Level 5"

# -- CRW v3.1 parser ----------------------------------------------------------
def parse_crw_header(lines):
    """
    Extract metadata from the CRW Virtual Station plain-text header block.

    The header has no comment markers -- it is free-form text followed by a
    space-delimited data table. We scan for sentinel strings to locate the MMM
    value and the data table start line.
    """
    meta = {}
    for i, line in enumerate(lines):
        stripped = line.strip()
        if stripped == "Averaged Maximum Monthly Mean:":
            meta["mmm"] = float(lines[i + 1].strip())
        if stripped == "Name:":
            meta["name"] = lines[i + 1].strip()
        if stripped.startswith("YYYY MM DD"):
            meta["data_start_line"] = i
            break
    return meta


def fetch_crw_station(site_name, station_id, force_refresh=False):
    """
    Fetch a NOAA CRW Virtual Station .txt file and return a tidy DataFrame.

    File format: multi-line plain-text header (no # markers), followed by a
    space-delimited data table with columns:
        YYYY MM DD SST_MIN SST_MAX SST@90th_HS SSTA@90th_HS 90th_HS>0
        DHW_from_90th_HS>1 BAA_7day_max

    The header contains the MMM baseline value which we extract and carry
    through as a site-level attribute. Caches the raw .txt file to disk to
    preserve the original header and MMM value across sessions.
    """
    cache_path = CACHE_DIR / f"{station_id}.txt"

    if not force_refresh and cache_path.exists():
        with open(cache_path, "r") as f:
            raw_lines = f.readlines()
    else:
        url = CRW_BASE_URL.format(station_id=station_id)
        resp = requests.get(url, timeout=30)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        raw_lines = resp.text.splitlines(keepends=True)
        with open(cache_path, "w") as f:
            f.writelines(raw_lines)

    meta = parse_crw_header(raw_lines)
    data_start = meta["data_start_line"]

    data_text = "".join(raw_lines[data_start:])
    df = pd.read_csv(
        StringIO(data_text),
        sep=r"\s+",
        na_values=["-999", "-9999", "-99.0000"],
    )

    df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(
        df["YYYY"].astype(str) + "-" +
        df["MM"].astype(str).str.zfill(2) + "-" +
        df["DD"].astype(str).str.zfill(2)
    )

    df.rename(columns={
        "SST@90th_HS":        "sst",
        "SSTA@90th_HS":       "sst_anom",
        "90th_HS>0":          "hotspot",
        "DHW_from_90th_HS>1": "dhw",
        "BAA_7day_max":       "alert",
    }, inplace=True)

    df["site"] = site_name
    df["mmm"]  = meta.get("mmm", None)

    keep = ["date", "sst", "sst_anom", "hotspot", "dhw", "alert", "site", "mmm"]
    df = df[[c for c in keep if c in df.columns]]
    df.sort_values("date", inplace=True)
    df.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)
    return df


def fetch_2026_ytd(site_name, station_id):
    """Fetch full station history, filter to 2026 YTD, always hits NOAA live."""
    df = fetch_crw_station(site_name, station_id, force_refresh=True)
    if df is None:
        return None
    mask = df["date"].dt.year == 2026
    sub = df[mask].copy()
    return sub if len(sub) > 0 else None


def fetch_oni():
    """
    Fetch NOAA CPC Oceanic Nino Index (ONI) data.

    Format: fixed-width text, columns SEAS YR TOTAL ANOM.
    SEAS is a 3-month season label (e.g. JFM, FMA, ..., DJF).
    ANOM is the SST anomaly in the Nino 3.4 region in deg C.
    We assign each season to the middle month for plotting.
    """
    SEASON_TO_MONTH = {
        "DJF": 1, "JFM": 2, "FMA": 3, "MAM": 4,
        "AMJ": 5, "MJJ": 6, "JJA": 7, "JAS": 8,
        "ASO": 9, "SON": 10, "OND": 11, "NDJ": 12,
    }
    resp = requests.get(ONI_URL, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    rows = []
    for line in resp.text.splitlines():
        parts = line.split()
        if len(parts) < 4 or parts[0] == "SEAS":
            continue
        try:
            seas  = parts[0]
            yr    = int(parts[1])
            anom  = float(parts[3])
            month = SEASON_TO_MONTH.get(seas)
            if month is None:
                continue
            rows.append({"year": yr, "month": month, "oni": anom})
        except ValueError:
            continue
    df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
    df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(
        df["year"].astype(str) + "-" +
        df["month"].astype(str).str.zfill(2) + "-01"
    )
    df.sort_values("date", inplace=True)
    df.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)
    return df
```

title: 2026 Super El Niño Watch: Live Thermal Stress Monitoring
text:
Overview
When I finished the first project — a historical analysis of coral bleaching risk using NOAA Coral Reef Watch DHW data — I had a natural next question. The historical record is clarifying: it shows what happened, when, and where. But coral bleaching is not a historical problem. It is happening right now, and 2026 is shaping up to be another year worth watching closely.
This project is the natural next step. Same data source. Same parser. I included the same five sites from the bleaching analysis, plus two additional Indo-Pacific sites — Palau and West Papua (Raja Ampat) — centered in the El Nino pattern. The difference is that instead of asking what happened during past bleaching events, I want to ask what is happening right now.
Think of it less as a finished analysis and more as a live instrument — the kind of thing a reef manager might bookmark and check weekly.
I am a data scientist learning marine science, not a coral ecologist. Everything here is derived from NOAA’s publicly available monitoring products, and I have linked the authoritative sources throughout. My goal is to read the data clearly and communicate it honestly.






Note



This page re-executes via daily GitHub Actions cron at 06:00 UTC. Data are fetched live from NOAA CRW on every render. Values shown reflect conditions as of the timestamp above.

section: A note on the data pipeline
text:
A note on the data pipeline
The first project deliberately kept implementation details in the background — the focus there was on the science, and a long digression on file parsing would have broken that flow. But since this project extends the same pipeline to a live monitoring context, it feels like the right moment to pull back the curtain a bit.
One of the more satisfying parts of building this project was writing a parser for NOAA CRW’s Virtual Station file format — and I’ll be honest: this was Claude’s contribution more than mine. My background is in structured clinical data — HL7 messages, FHIR resources, JSON pipelines — where the schema is always explicit, the delimiter is never ambiguous, and a malformed record throws a validation error before it ever reaches your code. The CRW format is a different animal entirely.
The files are plain-text, with a free-form prose header that looks like this:
Name:
Florida Keys
...
Averaged Maximum Monthly Mean:
28.36
...
YYYY MM DD SST_MIN SST_MAX SST@90th_HS SSTA@90th_HS 90th_HS>0 DHW_from_90th_HS>1 BAA_7day_max
2000 12 25  27.01   27.45   27.12        -1.24         0.00      0.00               0
No # comment markers. No separator line between header and data. Column names with @, >, and % characters that pandas will misread without explicit handling. The MMM value — the thermal baseline that the entire alert system is built on — is sitting two lines below a plain-text label, with no key-value structure to anchor it.
parse_crw_header() handles this by scanning the raw lines one at a time, looking for two sentinel strings: "Averaged Maximum Monthly Mean:" tells it the next line is the MMM float, and "YYYY MM DD" marks the exact line where the data table begins. That line index gets passed to pd.read_csv() via StringIO, so pandas never sees the header at all — it reads only the structured portion of the file.
The date column requires one more fix. The data table stores date as three separate integer columns — YYYY, MM, DD — which is common in older scientific datasets. The safest reconstruction is string concatenation with str.zfill(2) to zero-pad single-digit months and days before passing to pd.to_datetime(). Skipping the zero-padding produces silent failures on dates like January 5th (2026-1-5 parses inconsistently across pandas versions; 2026-01-05 does not).
Finally, fetch_crw_station() renames the v3.1 column names — DHW_from_90th_HS>1, SSTA@90th_HS, BAA_7day_max — to readable aliases (dhw, sst_anom, alert) and carries the MMM value through as a site-level column, so every row in the output DataFrame knows its own thermal baseline. That makes the alert classification logic in dhw_to_alert() self-contained and easy to audit.
This parser is carried over directly from the first project with two small changes: the cache now stores the raw .txt file rather than a parsed CSV (preserving the original header and MMM value across sessions), and the sst_anomaly column alias from the bleaching project is shortened to sst_anom here for consistency with the status table and plot code.
Output directories are anchored to the project root rather than using bare relative paths, so the cache and figure outputs from this project stay isolated from the bleaching project even if both kernels are running simultaneously.

section: El Niño progression and reef thermal stress
text:
El Niño progression and reef thermal stress
The anomalies in the chart above are not random variation — this project is called “2026 Super El Nino Watch” for a reason. The working hypothesis from the start has been that an active or developing El Nino event is driving elevated SSTs across our monitoring sites. What we haven’t done yet is show that directly.
It’s worth being explicit about this: starting with a hypothesis and then looking for supporting evidence is not the same as falsifying it. The honest next step is to bring in an independent measure of El Nino progression and ask whether the pattern we’re seeing at the reefs actually tracks it — and whether it doesn’t track it anywhere, which would be equally informative.
This is where the Oceanic Nino Index (ONI) comes in. The ONI is NOAA’s official metric for declaring and tracking El Nino and La Nina events. It measures SST anomalies in the Nino 3.4 region — a patch of the central equatorial Pacific that acts as the primary engine of ENSO variability. When the ONI stays at or above +0.5°C for five consecutive overlapping 3-month periods, NOAA officially declares an El Nino. I’m pretty sure I learned this in high school earth science — but I checked.
To be clear: the physical mechanism linking El Nino to coral bleaching is not in question, and I want to be honest about how I know that — I am not a marine scientist. It just seemed like a no-brainer from the data, so I did a literature search and read some abstracts. It turns out the causal chain is well established and has been for decades.
The 1998 global bleaching event, which killed an estimated 16% of the world’s coral reefs, coincided with one of the strongest El Nino events on record. The mechanism is documented: El Nino suppresses equatorial upwelling, reduces cloud cover, and drives anomalously warm surface waters across the Indo-Pacific warm pool, creating conditions for prolonged thermal stress to accumulate into dangerous DHW values. Hoegh-Guldberg (1999) formalized much of this. Hughes et al. (2017, 2018) documented it in devastating detail for the Great Barrier Reef during the 2016 event — back-to-back mass bleaching on a scale that had no historical precedent. The Caribbean connection is less direct mechanistically but is also documented; Eakin et al. (2010) covers the 2005 Caribbean bleaching event, which was the worst on record at the time.
I’ll be transparent about the limitation here: correlation between ONI and DHW by basin is suggestive at best. I’m married to an economist, and I would be remiss if I didn’t address the correlation-causation issue at hand.
She would probably start by pointing out that what we have here is a two-variable time series plot with no controls, no lag structure, and a sample size of one El Nino event. That’s not a finding — that’s a hypothesis. To do this properly she’d suggest something along the lines of:

Distributed lag models — the effect of ONI on reef SST anomalies isn’t instantaneous; it propagates over weeks to months depending on the basin. A proper analysis would test a range of lags (4, 8, 12 weeks) and report which lag structure best fits the data at each site.
Basin-separated regressions — ENSO affects each basin through different mechanisms and on different timescales. She’d run them separately.
Robustness checks — does the correlation hold if you drop the Florida Keys outlier? Does it hold across previous El Nino years in the historical record? If the relationship falls apart when you remove one site or one year, it isn’t robust.
Granger causality — does ONI actually help predict future DHW values, or are they just moving together for unrelated reasons? A Granger test wouldn’t establish physical causation but would at least tell us whether the ONI signal has predictive value for reef stress.

This came up at dinner. She’s right. This is a monitoring dashboard, not a causal study, and the chart below should be read as a visual sanity check against a known climate driver — not as evidence of a causal relationship. A rigorous treatment is on the list for a future version of this project.
One more honest note: I started this project because I dive these reefs and care about what happens to them. The Florida Keys anomaly, West Papua already carrying DHW, all seven sites at Bleaching Watch before summer even starts in the Northern Hemisphere — I know what that pattern looked like in 2023 and 2016. I’m prepared for some serious heartbreak this summer. I hope I’m wrong.
One data note worth flagging: the ONI is published with a lag of one to two months by NOAA’s Climate Prediction Center. The most recent bar in the chart below may not reflect current conditions. For the most current ENSO status, NOAA CPC publishes weekly updates at https://www.cpc.ncep.noaa.gov/products/analysis_monitoring/enso_advisory/index.shtml. This lag also explains why the ONI bars may end earlier than the DHW lines — the CRW v3.1 Virtual Station data is updated daily, making it considerably more current than the ONI’s monthly publication schedule.

def basin_mean_dhw(site_list):

        return pd.DataFrame(columns=["date", "mean_dhw"])
    return (
        pd.concat(frames)
        .groupby("date")["dhw"]
        .mean()
        .reset_index()
        .rename(columns={"dhw": "mean_dhw"})

caribbean_dhw   = basin_mean_dhw(caribbean_sites)

                 alpha=0.12, color="#1B5E20")
ax2.set_ylabel("Mean DHW by basin (deg C-wk)", fontsize=10, color="#424242")
ax2.tick_params(axis="y", labelcolor="#424242")
ax2.set_ylim(0, max(
    caribbean_dhw["mean_dhw"].max() if len(caribbean_dhw) > 0 else 0,

) * 2.2 or 8)

lines1, labels1 = ax1.get_legend_handles_labels()
lines2, labels2 = ax2.get_legend_handles_labels()

           loc="upper left", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)

ax1.tick_params(axis="x", labelsize=8, rotation=30)
fig.suptitle(
    f"Oceanic Nino Index vs. Mean Reef DHW by Basin -- 2026 YTD\n"
    f"ONI source: NOAA Climate Prediction Center  |  "
    f"DHW: NOAA Coral Reef Watch v3.1  |  as of {fetch_stamp}",

The chart above is worth reading carefully. The Indo-Pacific DHW peak in January-February reflects the Southern Hemisphere summer ending at GBR Central and West Papua — not a response to El Nino onset. The ONI transition from negative to positive in March-April is the signal to watch. If that positive ONI value holds and strengthens through Northern Hemisphere summer, the Caribbean sites — currently at DHW zero but with anomalies already well above the bleaching threshold — are the ones most at risk for rapid stress accumulation. Check back in July.

title: 2026 Super El Niño Watch: Live Thermal Stress Monitoring
text:
Interpretation & limitations
The data as of mid-2026 tells a consistent story: every reef system in this analysis is already running above its historical thermal baseline, and two Indo-Pacific sites are carrying residual heat stress from a Southern Hemisphere summer that just ended. The ONI has crossed into positive territory. Northern Hemisphere bleaching season has not yet begun.
That combination — anomalies already elevated, ONI turning positive, DHW near zero but primed — is consistent with the early-season setup observed before the 2015-2016 and 2023 bleaching events. Whether it follows the same trajectory depends on how the ONI evolves over the next 60-90 days and whether atmospheric conditions allow that heat to accumulate at the reef surface rather than being mixed away.
A few honest limitations to keep in mind when reading this page:
Spatial resolution. The CRW v3.1 Virtual Station data is derived from a 5 km satellite product. That is a significant improvement over the legacy 50 km product, but it is still a regional average. Conditions at a specific reef within that pixel may differ meaningfully from what the satellite measures.
Single pixel per site. Each site in this analysis is represented by one Virtual Station — one pixel. The Great Barrier Reef alone spans 2,300 km. GBR Central is a reasonable representative but it is not the whole reef.
SST is not the whole story. DHW measures surface thermal stress. It does not capture light intensity, water clarity, local acidification, prior bleaching history, or the adaptive capacity of individual coral populations. Two reefs with identical DHW values can have very different bleaching outcomes depending on their ecological condition and thermal history.
This is not a forecast. Nothing in this notebook predicts what will happen. It describes current conditions and flags patterns that, based on historical precedent, warrant attention.
The ONI correlation is suggestive, not causal at the site level. See the El Nino section above for the full treatment of this limitation.

title: 2026 Super El Niño Watch: Live Thermal Stress Monitoring
text:
Future directions
Building this notebook taught me something I did not fully anticipate going in. At some point while I was wrangling the CRW parser, figuring out why the GBR station ID was wrong, and trying to decide whether to pool Caribbean and Indo-Pacific DHW into the same mean — I realized this is just a data integration problem. Different sources, different schemas, different update frequencies, different levels of trust. You have to decide what to join on, how to handle missing data, and what the right unit of analysis is.
That is my day job. I work in biomedical and clinical research informatics. I do this with HL7 messages and FHIR resources and clinical trial datasets. It turns out the problem is not unique to healthcare — a reef manager trying to assess bleaching risk at a site they are responsible for is facing exactly the same challenge, just with satellite products instead of EHR extracts.
That realization is what makes the following feel less like a wish list and more like a natural next project.
Toward a multi-factor bleaching risk score:
A composite bleaching risk score that goes beyond DHW is not a new idea. NOAA CRW already publishes an experimental Four-Month Coral Bleaching Outlook (Liu et al., 2018), updated weekly, which forecasts the probability of bleaching-level heat stress using SST projections from NOAA’s Climate Forecast System Version 2 (CFSv2) ensemble. It is the closest published analog to what I am describing here — but it is still purely thermal. It does not incorporate light stress, water quality, acidification, or ecological condition.
There is also published work on taxon-specific Bleaching Response Indices that standardize susceptibility across coral species (Marshall & Baird, 2000), and peer-reviewed evidence that bleaching predictions improve substantially when interannual SST variability and coral thermal history are included alongside raw DHW values (Thompson & Dolman, 2010).
The gap this future direction targets is integrating those additional stressors into a site-level risk score that a reef manager could actually act on. The data streams that would need to come together:

Photosynthetically active radiation (PAR) — light intensity at the reef surface compounds thermal stress significantly. NOAA CRW publishes a satellite-derived PAR product that could be integrated directly into the existing pipeline.
Water clarity and turbidity — MODIS and VIIRS ocean color products provide estimates of chlorophyll concentration and diffuse attenuation coefficient (Kd490), which serve as proxies for water clarity.
Ocean acidification — NOAA’s Ocean Acidification Program publishes aragonite saturation state estimates for tropical reef regions. Corals under chronic acidification stress have reduced capacity to recover from bleaching events, making acidification a risk multiplier rather than an independent stressor.
Prior bleaching history — the NOAA CoRIS and ReefBase databases contain georeferenced bleaching observation records that could be used to construct a site-level bleaching history index.
Thermal history and adaptive capacity — there is growing evidence that corals with prior exposure to variable thermal environments develop greater heat tolerance. A rough proxy could be derived from the CoralTemp historical record: reefs with high interannual SST variability over the past two decades may harbor more thermally tolerant populations. This is contested in the literature but tractable from data we already have.
DHW trajectory — the rate of DHW accumulation over the past two to four weeks. A reef at 3 DHW-wk and climbing fast is at higher near-term risk than a reef at 3 DHW-wk that has been stable for three weeks.
In-water validation — integrating citizen science reports from CoralWatch, Reef Check, and NOAA’s National Coral Reef Monitoring Program would substantially improve ecological validity.

From a data engineering standpoint this is a familiar problem: each of these sources has a different spatial resolution, a different update frequency, a different file format, and a different level of provenance documentation. Making them interoperable at the site level is exactly the kind of pipeline work this project is already doing in miniature — and exactly the kind of problem my background in clinical data integration is well suited to.
Other near-term directions:

Add historical DHW context — overlay 2026 YTD against the same period in 2015, 2016, and 2023 for each site
Basin-separated ONI regression with lag testing
Integrate NOAA’s CFS-based Bleaching Outlook probabilities as a third data source alongside the ONI and DHW
Expand to additional Indo-Pacific sites, particularly in the Coral Triangle

A personal note: I built this project to learn, to practice working with real environmental data pipelines, and because I dive these reefs and care about what happens to them. If you are a reef scientist, conservation organization, or research program working in any of these regions and find this work interesting or useful, I would genuinely welcome the conversation. title: Exploring Coral Bleaching Risk Using NOAA Coral Reef Watch DHW Data
text:
When I started looking for a dataset to anchor this first marine science project, I had a simple question: what data actually gets used by the people trying to protect coral reefs in the real world? That question led me directly to NOAA’s Coral Reef Watch (CRW) program and their Degree Heating Weeks (DHW) product — and then to the paper that describes exactly how it’s built: Skirving et al. (2020), CoralTemp and the Coral Reef Watch Coral Bleaching Heat Stress Product Suite Version 3.1 (Skirving et al. 2020).
Reading that paper changed how I thought about this project.






Data source: NOAA Coral Reef Watch Version 3.1 Daily 5km Satellite Coral Bleaching Heat Stress Product Suite. Updated daily. coralreefwatch.noaa.gov





My first instinct was to pull raw SST data — it’s widely available from multiple sources including NOAA OISST, NASA MODIS, and the Copernicus Marine Service. But raw SST alone doesn’t tell you whether a coral is stressed. A reading of 29°C means something very different on the Great Barrier Reef versus Bonaire. What matters isn’t the absolute temperature — it’s how that temperature compares to what the corals at that location have adapted to over their lifetimes.
That realization pushed me toward NOAA CRW’s derived product suite, where that comparison is already built in — and rigorously validated against decades of field bleaching observations worldwide.



The entire CRW product suite rests on a climatological baseline: a set of long-term average SST values for every location on Earth, for every month of the year. This is not a trivial thing to build correctly. As Skirving et al. (2020) describe, CRW spent years grappling with a fundamental problem — the satellite SST data used to build the climatology came from different sensors and processing pipelines than the near-real-time data used to calculate the anomalies. That mismatch introduced systematic biases, particularly at the high temperatures most relevant to bleaching.
The development of CoralTemp — a single, continuous daily SST record spanning 1985 to the present, built by carefully blending three different satellite products — was specifically designed to solve this problem. For the first time, CRW could compute the climatology and the daily anomalies from the same underlying dataset. The Monthly Mean (MM) climatology was derived from this record across 1985–2012, and the Maximum Monthly Mean (MMM) — the warmest month at each location — became the critical threshold from which the HotSpot and DHW products are derived.
I want to be transparent about why this matters for this project: I am not independently validating the baseline. I am trusting that NOAA CRW’s 25-year investment in getting this right is sound — which is supported by the peer-reviewed literature and by the fact that these products are the global standard used by reef managers worldwide. But understanding that the baseline exists, how it was constructed, and what its assumptions are is essential context for interpreting everything downstream. The DHW values we analyze are only as meaningful as the MMM they’re derived from.



One more thing worth acknowledging upfront: the Virtual Station data used in this analysis represents a single 5×5 km satellite pixel per reef location. Real reef systems are far more heterogeneous than that. Temperatures vary with depth, local current patterns, reef orientation, and bathymetry — a coral colony in a deep channel experiences very different conditions than one on a shallow exposed crest, even within the same reef system.
This means the DHW values we analyze are best understood as a reef-wide surface thermal stress index — a strong and validated predictor of bleaching risk at the population level, but not a precise measurement of conditions at any specific location. This is the same limitation the entire global reef monitoring community works with, and it doesn’t undermine the analysis. It just means we should be careful about over-interpreting site-level differences that might reflect local thermal refugia or exposure patterns rather than true differences in bleaching pressure.
With those caveats clearly on the table, here is what this project actually does.

title: Exploring Coral Bleaching Risk Using NOAA Coral Reef Watch DHW Data
section: Why This Work Matters to Me
text:
Why This Work Matters to Me
The spikes in the DHW charts are not abstract. Each one represents a mass bleaching event — weeks of accumulated heat stress that caused corals to expel the symbiotic algae that give them both their color and their ability to survive. Some recovered. Many did not.
The 2023 spike on the Florida Keys chart stopped me the first time I saw it. That summer, sea surface temperatures off the Keys reached levels never recorded in the satellite era — DHW values pushing well past the Alert Level 2 threshold where mortality becomes likely. I did not feel unsettled. I felt implicated. Like I was watching something irreplaceable disappear and I was not doing my share to stop it.
We named this planet wrong. It is not Earth. It is Ocean. The ocean covers 71% of the surface, produces more than half the oxygen we breathe, and absorbs the heat that our atmosphere can no longer contain. Coral reefs are less than 1% of the ocean floor and support roughly 25% of all marine species. They are disproportionately vital and disproportionately vulnerable — and they are bleaching at a pace that the satellite record has never seen before.
That feeling of personal responsibility is what brought me here.
I have spent my career in biomedical and clinical research informatics — building data pipelines, managing research data governance, working with complex health datasets in high-stakes environments. The technical skills transfer. What I am building now is the domain knowledge and the credibility to point those skills at a different set of urgent problems. The ocean is not separate from human health. It is the precondition for it.
The monitoring infrastructure that NOAA CRW has built — the same data we are working with in this notebook — exists because early warning matters. A reef manager who knows a bleaching event is coming two weeks in advance can act. That connection between data, prediction, and real-world response is something I understand from clinical informatics. I want to bring it here. I want my work to matter outside the walls of a hospital system.
This project is a first step. The code is simple, the dataset is publicly available, and the science belongs to the researchers at NOAA CRW and the marine biologists doing fieldwork on these reefs. But the ability to work with this data rigorously, document it honestly, and communicate it clearly — that is something I can contribute. And I intend to keep going.

These sites were chosen to represent geographic diversity across two ocean basins, a range of documented bleaching histories, and contrasting thermal stress profiles. Station IDs were verified directly against the live NOAA CRW Virtual Station list at coralreefwatch.noaa.gov/product/vs/data.php before any data was fetched.






A note on the GBR: Unlike the Caribbean sites, the Great Barrier Reef has no single regional station — it is simply too large. NOAA splits it into five sectors: Torres Strait, Far Northern, Northern, Central, and Southern. We selected GBR Central, which covers the Whitsundays and Townsville region. My awareness of the 2016 bleaching event in this sector came initially from watching Jeff Orlowski’s documentary Chasing Coral — and I later found that the same event was the subject of Hughes et al. (2017) in Nature. The documentary and the peer-reviewed literature were pointing at the same place.

title: Exploring Coral Bleaching Risk Using NOAA Coral Reef Watch DHW Data
text:
Science Background

Sea Surface Temperature Anomaly
Every ocean location has a long-term average temperature for each time of year, derived from decades of satellite records. The SST anomaly measures the departure of today’s temperature from that historical average:
\[\text{SST Anomaly} = \text{SST} - \text{Daily SST Climatology}\]
A positive anomaly means the ocean is warmer than usual for that season. This metric provides broad context for ocean warming but is not on its own a reliable predictor of coral bleaching, because corals in different locations are adapted to different baseline temperatures.


Coral Bleaching HotSpot
The HotSpot product is calibrated specifically to coral biology. It measures how far the current temperature exceeds the warmest month that corals at that location have historically experienced — a threshold called the Maximum Monthly Mean (MMM):
\[\text{HotSpot} = \text{SST} - \text{MMM SST Climatology}, \quad \text{HotSpot} \geq 0\]
A HotSpot ≥ 1°C indicates active thermal stress. The MMM baseline varies by location, so this metric captures relative stress rather than absolute temperature — making it comparable across sites with very different ambient ocean temperatures (Skirving et al. 2020).


Degree Heating Weeks (DHW)
A single warm day can stress corals, but corals are resilient to brief exposures. What causes bleaching is sustained thermal stress accumulating over weeks. Degree Heating Weeks integrate the HotSpot values (when ≥ 1°C) over a moving 12-week window:
\[\text{DHW} = \frac{\sum_{d=0}^{83} \max(\text{HotSpot}_d - 0, 0) \cdot \mathbf{1}[\text{HotSpot}_d \geq 1]}{7}\]
Units are °C-weeks. Think of DHW as a “heat debt” that builds up as hot conditions persist — analogous to the accumulated dose concept in clinical pharmacology or radiation biology.
Two key thresholds, established through satellite monitoring and field validation (Liu et al. 2013):








DHW Value
Alert Level
Biological Expectation




< 4 °C-weeks
Watch / Warning
Thermal stress present; monitoring needed


≥ 4 °C-weeks
Alert Level 1
Bleaching likely


≥ 8 °C-weeks
Alert Level 2
Severe bleaching; significant mortality likely

title: Exploring Coral Bleaching Risk Using NOAA Coral Reef Watch DHW Data
text:
Data & Methods

Data Acquisition
Data were fetched programmatically from the NOAA CRW Virtual Station feed using verified station IDs. The fetch URL pattern is:
https://coralreefwatch.noaa.gov/product/vs/data/{station_id}.txt
Each file opens with an unstructured metadata header containing station-level information — including the Averaged Maximum Monthly Mean (MMM) temperature, which we extract programmatically and carry through the analysis as the location-specific bleaching threshold baseline.






A note on the file format: The NOAA CRW Virtual Station files are not simple CSVs. The header uses plain text labels rather than # comment markers, the date is split across three columns (YYYY, MM, DD), and the column names reflect the updated v3.1 product suite. We inspected the raw file before writing the parser rather than assuming a format — a step worth documenting for anyone reproducing this analysis.



Data are cached locally after first retrieval. To force a fresh download, set force_refresh=True in the fetch call.


Analysis Pipeline

Fetch — live HTTP request to CRW Virtual Station feed per site
Cache — store raw .txt files in data/raw/
Parse — extract MMM from header, build date column, rename columns
Visualize — DHW time series, alert level distributions, annual peak heatmap
Interpret — narrative analysis of key bleaching events

title: Exploring Coral Bleaching Risk Using NOAA Coral Reef Watch DHW Data
text:
Results


Setup: imports, directories, thresholds
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import requests
import os
from io import StringIO

# Paths relative to projects/ directory where this .qmd lives
CACHE_DIR = "data/raw"
FIG_DIR   = "figures"
os.makedirs(CACHE_DIR, exist_ok=True)
os.makedirs(FIG_DIR,   exist_ok=True)

# Bleaching thresholds (Skirving et al. 2020, Liu et al. 2013)
DHW_WARNING = 4   # Alert Level 1: Bleaching Likely
DHW_SEVERE  = 8   # Alert Level 2: Mortality Likely




Study site definitions and verified NOAA CRW station IDs
SITES = {
    "Florida Keys": {
        "station_id": "florida_keys",
        "lat": 24.55,
        "lon": -81.50,
        "ocean": "Caribbean/Atlantic",
    },
    "Cayman Islands": {
        "station_id": "cayman_islands",
        "lat": 19.30,
        "lon": -81.38,
        "ocean": "Caribbean",
    },
    "Honduras (Bay Islands)": {
        "station_id": "honduras",
        "lat": 16.32,
        "lon": -86.55,
        "ocean": "Caribbean",
    },
    "ABC Islands (Bonaire)": {
        "station_id": "abc_islands",
        "lat": 12.15,
        "lon": -68.28,
        "ocean": "Caribbean",
    },
    "GBR Central": {
        "station_id": "gbr_central",
        "lat": -18.00,
        "lon": 147.00,
        "ocean": "Indo-Pacific",
    },
}

for name, cfg in SITES.items():
    print(f"✓ {name:30} | station: {cfg['station_id']}")


✓ Florida Keys                   | station: florida_keys
✓ Cayman Islands                 | station: cayman_islands
✓ Honduras (Bay Islands)         | station: honduras
✓ ABC Islands (Bonaire)          | station: abc_islands
✓ GBR Central                    | station: gbr_central




Fetch and parse NOAA CRW Virtual Station data
CRW_BASE_URL = "https://coralreefwatch.noaa.gov/product/vs/data/{station_id}.txt"

def parse_crw_header(lines):
    """Extract metadata from the CRW file header block."""
    meta = {}
    for i, line in enumerate(lines):
        line_stripped = line.strip()
        if line_stripped == "Averaged Maximum Monthly Mean:":
            meta["mmm"] = float(lines[i + 1].strip())
        if line_stripped == "Name:":
            meta["name"] = lines[i + 1].strip()
        if line_stripped.startswith("YYYY MM DD"):
            meta["data_start_line"] = i
            break
    return meta

def fetch_crw_station(site_name, station_id, force_refresh=False):
    cache_path = os.path.join(CACHE_DIR, f"{station_id}.txt")

    if not force_refresh and os.path.exists(cache_path):
        print(f"  ✓ Loaded from cache: {site_name}")
        with open(cache_path, "r") as f:
            raw_lines = f.readlines()
    else:
        url = CRW_BASE_URL.format(station_id=station_id)
        print(f"  ↓ Fetching live: {site_name}")
        resp = requests.get(url, timeout=30)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        raw_lines = resp.text.splitlines(keepends=True)
        with open(cache_path, "w") as f:
            f.writelines(raw_lines)

    meta = parse_crw_header(raw_lines)
    data_start = meta["data_start_line"]

    data_text = "".join(raw_lines[data_start:])
    df = pd.read_csv(
        StringIO(data_text),
        sep=r"\s+",
        na_values=["-999", "-9999", "-99.0000"],
    )

    df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(
        df["YYYY"].astype(str) + "-" +
        df["MM"].astype(str).str.zfill(2) + "-" +
        df["DD"].astype(str).str.zfill(2)
    )

    df.rename(columns={
        "SST@90th_HS":        "sst",
        "SSTA@90th_HS":       "sst_anomaly",
        "90th_HS>0":          "hotspot",
        "DHW_from_90th_HS>1": "dhw",
        "BAA_7day_max":       "alert",
    }, inplace=True)

    df["site"] = site_name
    df["mmm"]  = meta.get("mmm", None)

    keep = ["date", "sst", "sst_anomaly", "hotspot", "dhw", "alert", "site", "mmm"]
    df = df[[c for c in keep if c in df.columns]]
    df.sort_values("date", inplace=True)
    df.reset_index(drop=True, inplace=True)

    print(f"    ✓ {len(df):,} rows | "
          f"{df['date'].min().date()} → {df['date'].max().date()} | "
          f"MMM: {meta.get('mmm')}°C")
    return df


print("Fetching NOAA CRW Virtual Station data...\n")
all_data = {}
for site_name, cfg in SITES.items():
    df = fetch_crw_station(site_name, cfg["station_id"])
    all_data[site_name] = df

print(f"\n✓ Done. {len(all_data)} sites loaded.")


Fetching NOAA CRW Virtual Station data...

  ✓ Loaded from cache: Florida Keys
    ✓ 15,105 rows | 1985-01-01 → 2026-05-10 | MMM: 29.6264°C
  ✓ Loaded from cache: Cayman Islands
    ✓ 15,105 rows | 1985-01-01 → 2026-05-10 | MMM: 29.3925°C
  ✓ Loaded from cache: Honduras (Bay Islands)
    ✓ 15,105 rows | 1985-01-01 → 2026-05-10 | MMM: 28.87°C
  ✓ Loaded from cache: ABC Islands (Bonaire)
    ✓ 15,105 rows | 1985-01-01 → 2026-05-10 | MMM: 28.0193°C
  ✓ Loaded from cache: GBR Central
    ✓ 15,105 rows | 1985-01-01 → 2026-05-10 | MMM: 28.3422°C

✓ Done. 5 sites loaded.



MMM Baseline Values
The MMM values extracted from the file headers reveal the location-specific thermal baselines — before a single chart is plotted:


MMM baseline values per site
mmm_rows = [
    {"Site": name, "MMM (°C)": df["mmm"].iloc[0]}
    for name, df in all_data.items()
]
mmm_df = pd.DataFrame(mmm_rows).sort_values("MMM (°C)", ascending=False)
mmm_df["MMM (°C)"] = mmm_df["MMM (°C)"].map("{:.2f}".format)
print(mmm_df.to_string(index=False))


                  Site MMM (°C)
          Florida Keys    29.63
        Cayman Islands    29.39
Honduras (Bay Islands)    28.87
           GBR Central    28.34
 ABC Islands (Bonaire)    28.02


This is the MMM doing exactly what Skirving et al. (2020) described — capturing location-specific thermal tolerance rather than absolute temperature. A raw SST reading of 29°C represents no stress at all in the Florida Keys, but would already be pushing past the bleaching threshold on the GBR.


DHW Time Series
The figure below shows Degree Heating Week time series for all five reef sites from 2000 to present. Horizontal dashed lines mark the Alert Level 1 (4 °C-weeks) and Alert Level 2 (8 °C-weeks) thresholds. Each panel also shows the site’s MMM baseline temperature.


DHW time series — all five sites
fig, axes = plt.subplots(
    nrows=len(all_data), ncols=1,
    figsize=(14, 3.2 * len(all_data)),
    sharex=False
)

colors = ["#0077b6", "#00b4d8", "#48cae4", "#90e0ef", "#e07a5f"]

for ax, (site, df), color in zip(axes, all_data.items(), colors):
    mask = df["date"] >= "2000-01-01"
    plot_df = df[mask].copy()

    ax.fill_between(plot_df["date"], plot_df["dhw"],
                    alpha=0.25, color=color)
    ax.plot(plot_df["date"], plot_df["dhw"],
            color=color, lw=1.0, label="DHW")

    ax.axhline(DHW_WARNING, color="#f4a261", ls="--", lw=1.2,
               label=f"Alert L1 ({DHW_WARNING} °C-wks)")
    ax.axhline(DHW_SEVERE, color="#e63946", ls="--", lw=1.2,
               label=f"Alert L2 ({DHW_SEVERE} °C-wks)")

    ax.set_title(
        f"{site}  |  MMM: {df['mmm'].iloc[0]:.2f}°C",
        fontsize=11, fontweight="bold", loc="left"
    )
    ax.set_ylabel("DHW (°C-weeks)", fontsize=9)
    ax.set_ylim(bottom=0)
    ax.legend(fontsize=7, loc="upper left", framealpha=0.7)
    ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.25)
    ax.spines[["top", "right"]].set_visible(False)

axes[-1].set_xlabel("Date", fontsize=10)
fig.suptitle(
    "NOAA CRW Degree Heating Weeks by Reef Site (2000–present)\n"
    "Data: NOAA Coral Reef Watch Version 3.1",
    fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", y=1.01
)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig(os.path.join(FIG_DIR, "dhw_timeseries_all_sites.png"),
            dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()








Figure 1: DHW time series for all five reef sites (2000–present). Dashed lines mark bleaching alert thresholds. MMM values shown in panel titles. Data: NOAA CRW Version 3.1.





Key observations:

Florida Keys — the 2023 spike dwarfs everything before it in the satellite record, consistent with the unprecedented marine heat wave that summer.
GBR Central — the 2016 and 2022 events are clearly visible; the lower MMM baseline means bleaching stress kicks in at a lower absolute temperature.
ABC Islands (Bonaire) — spikes are consistently lower than the other Caribbean sites, consistent with its reputation for relatively robust reef health.
All Caribbean sites — show a clear pattern of increasing frequency and magnitude of DHW exceedances from 2015 onward.

title: Exploring Coral Bleaching Risk Using NOAA Coral Reef Watch DHW Data
text:
Interpretation & Limitations

What this analysis shows
This analysis demonstrates the utility of satellite thermal monitoring for tracking bleaching risk across geographically distributed reef systems. The DHW metric — developed and validated by NOAA CRW scientists over more than 20 years — provides a biologically grounded, globally consistent index of accumulated thermal stress that has been correlated with field observations of bleaching onset and severity.


Limitations to note

Point data: CRW Virtual Station data represent a single 5×5 km pixel per site. Actual reef conditions vary at finer spatial scales due to bathymetry, currents, and local upwelling.
No in situ validation: This analysis does not include field bleaching surveys or coral cover data. DHW is a risk indicator, not a direct measurement of bleaching.
Alert thresholds are global: The 4 and 8 °C-week thresholds are population- level averages. Some coral communities may have higher or lower thermal tolerance.
GBR representation: The gbr_central station represents one of five GBR sectors. Thermal stress patterns vary considerably across the reef’s 2,300 km length.

title: Exploring Coral Bleaching Risk Using NOAA Coral Reef Watch DHW Data
text:
Future Directions
This MVP analysis establishes the data pipeline and visualization framework. Planned extensions include:

All five GBR sectors — compare Torres Strait, Far Northern, Northern, Central, and Southern to examine latitudinal bleaching gradients
Spatial raster analysis — process global CRW NetCDF files via ERDDAP to produce regional DHW maps
Bleaching observation integration — overlay NOAA CoRIS or Reef Check field survey data to validate satellite signals against in-water observations
ENSO correlation — correlate DHW peaks with Niño 3.4 SST anomaly index
Trend analysis — fit linear trend models to annual peak DHW to quantify warming trajectories at each site

text:
About This Project
This project is part of a portfolio in marine environmental data science, developed to demonstrate reproducible analysis workflows applied to real-world reef conservation data. The full code, data pipeline, and environment specification are available on GitHub.






TipReproducibility



This page renders live from embedded Python — data is fetched from NOAA CRW, parsed, and plotted on every render unless frozen. Outputs are frozen and committed to the repository via freeze: true. To re-execute from scratch, delete _freeze/projects/bleaching-risk-notebook/ from the portfolio site repo and re-render. The uv environment is fully specified in the coral-bleaching-dhw repository’s requirements.txt.


View source repo NOAA Coral Reef Watch

title: About Me
text:
I’m a clinical informaticist and data systems professional based in the Washington, D.C. area, currently working at the National Institutes of Health. My work sits at the intersection of clinical research, data science, primary data collection, and research infrastructure.
I work shoulder to shoulder with principal investigators, research teams, and operational stakeholders to understand scientific needs and translate them into usable systems, workflows, and data platforms. I also work with program and organizational leadership to shape enterprise data capabilities that support governance, interoperability, reproducibility, and long-term scientific value.
My professional background includes clinical research informatics, data governance, cloud-based research platforms, REDCap operations, metadata workflows, and support for large-scale scientific data programs.
I’m currently expanding my scope into marine science, GIS, remote sensing, Structure-from-Motion photogrammetry, full-stack software development, and hardware engineering. Long term, I’m interested in applying informatics, reproducible analysis, and geospatial tools to coral reef research and marine conservation at institutions such as Scripps Institution of Oceanography, Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution, Mote Marine Laboratory, NOAA, or similar research organizations.
Outside of work, I apply the same data and engineering mindset to cycling analytics, high-power rocketry, ham radio, Arduino-based systems, and embedded radio telemetry projects.
